Hi, welcome back to the celebrity branding blueprint. My name is Greg Rowlet and once again, I am joined by Emmy Award winning director, best selling author, Mr. Nick Nannon. And it is time to roll this thing out, isn't it? Yes, there's so much of the other stuff you can do creating your position and everything, kind of from your underwear and your back bedroom. But at some point now, where the rubber meets the road, where you start making money or you don't, is when you start rolling out. One of the funny things I often see, I'll draw a parallel to the music industry because we've both done a lot of work there and people have all heard bands and stuff. I have musicians who come to me and they'll say, hey Nick, I just created this CD, check it out. And surprisingly it's great. Let's see, okay, this is, wow, it's awesome. Okay, and they'll say, so what do I do next? So I give them steps one through 10 on what they should do. And then I don't hear from them for six months. They come back and say, hey Nick, check it out, I've got four new songs. I'm like, well that's great, but I told you the first five were good and still no one owns those because you didn't do anything to market, but you didn't roll out. That's a similar thing here. There's marketing and getting out there and actually selling. A lot of us sometimes are uncomfortable with that. We just don't know how to do it. We're really great at what we do, the widget thing we created, but then now it's time to get out there and if you don't take this step, you're never gonna make any money, so it's gonna be unsustainable. And I won't be able to help you help more people. Right, we totally see, so the drawing the parallel of the music industry is great is I give you the CD of six great songs and instead of going and marketing those six great songs, I make six more great songs. And now I got 12 great songs but still no fans. Well in business, same thing, right? So social media, great thing. I can tweet, Facebook and write blog posts all day. And so now I got three years worth of these blog posts and I'm like, Nick, I don't have any customers. It's because you never sold anything. You never marketed to these people. So this- Well by saying that word, no matter what business we think we might be in, like making music or writing books, whatever what business do we all really need to be in, Greg? It's the marketing business. And I think that it's the business of you is really what you are and it's the marketing of you. And I think that, again, people get caught up in being a cog is like, I'm a dentist. No, you're a marketer of dental services. David Ogilvy, great advertising legend, said that you can't save souls in an empty church. And again, you're preaching, but if you have no audience with which to preach to, then it falls on deaf ears and did the tree really fall if you didn't hear the tree fall. You know, who knows? So again, you're now the expert, right? At this stage, if you have a hundred blog posts or you're the best dentist in the world, you're the college professor in the basement, you know, that no one comes to visit because you're the scary, hairy, you know, dude, right? But so now how do we begin to change our mindset to marketing first? And where do we even begin? I mean, most people are just stumped there because you can go a million directions. Right. So we talked about creating a position. Now, one of the things we also talk briefly about is that most of your clients have no idea what you really know. Right. Excuse me. Most of your clients have no idea what you really know. Now, I love to do this. I'll talk about the fact that, first of all, we're all experts at something. For instance, you might think, well, I just do what I do. Well, there's likely something in what you do that's more efficient or better at getting a certain result than someone else in your same field. Right. For instance, an example I like to tell because people is hard to believe is that, okay, so we had a client who's a chiropractor and chiropractor wrote this ad. He was a great student of marketing. He understood he needed to be marketing in chiropractic services more so than just doing the chiropractic. He did both. He wrote this ad and I'm gonna make up numbers to not know the real numbers, but for instance, every time he ran into community paper, he'd get a hundred calls. Those 150 book appointments, 20 would come in and 10 would become a client. Let's just say that. So every hundred calls, every time he ran the paper, he got 10 clients. It was the ultimate response. So every time he ran it, he'd get 10 more clients. Well, he wrote a couple more community papers. He figured out the formula and he knew, every time I run these ads, here's what it cost me, here's what I bring in. It was a scalable, repeatable process for bringing in new clients. So even though he's an expert on chiropractic, he's now an expert on marketing chiropractic with one ad, right, one ad. So what do you do? He created a licensing system for him where he went out and he sold the rights to run this ad to a hundred different chiropractors in a hundred different communities. They paid him $1,000 a month. So a hundred grand a month to run this ad. So if you think your expertise isn't that valuable, most people would have said, well, I don't have that much value. I don't know that much. I just know how to run this ad. Well, if you know how to run that ad and bring in consistent results, that was worth to him with zero overhead practically, a million to a year in just letting people run his ad. And we wrote the contract. So I can assure you, if they stopped paying, they could not run the ad anymore. Yeah, I love that. So let's go to, so a lot of points you covered there. And I want to get to that, creating the second business. Cause I think that's where a lot of experts want to get to is they want to not just be the chiropractor, they want to be the Dr. Phil, right, type personality, which is where I want to get to. But first we got to run this first ad, right? And, you know, we could run a Facebook ad. We can buy, you know, community paper space. We could buy newspaper space, magazine ads. We can get a mailing list. I mean, the list goes on and on and on. You'll have a booth. You can do anything, but okay. So the next step is once you figure out where you're going to start marketing, what you have to do in order to get people to understand your expertise, you've got to start generating content. It's just as simple as that. The more content you have, the more of an expert you have. The more people can read your story, become a part of your story, the more they can get involved. So we utilize lots of formats that we use blogs, we use articles, we use press releases, we use books, we use DVDs, we use CDs, we use special reports, tell seminars, webinars, but all of that content together starts to tell our story and position us as truly a celebrity. We've talked on piece of this, but that's the rollout. It's taking my position and now starting to generate pieces of content that validate my position. Again, different than PR. The PR needs to validate who you say you are. But this is all who do I say I am. What are the stories? What are the people I'm helping? Who are the people I'm helping? What am I doing for them? How's this going? What are my philosophies? What's your business trifecta in your secret format for me to success? So you've got to start developing content. And believe me, this didn't all come together overnight in one minute. And we've been in business for quite a few years and even though I was talking about a lot of these concepts, they really, the more I talked about them, the more I questions out, the more they would really solidify. I get questions that, again, when you don't know where to start with generating content, it's because you know as much as you know. You don't know where to start. Well, start at the beginning. There's a ton of people who I thought it was obvious you needed to have media marketing in PR, let's just say for example. Had we not talked that to begin with, no one would know where we're coming from. But I didn't think that was something to start talking about. So I get these questions. I'd say, well, it's really kind of like this. And believe me, it wasn't that eloquent. At first it wasn't the trifecta, the media marketing PR. I get more questions that have to be kind of like, well, you know, what you're really missing seems to be the marketing, but you have the media and PR, or you have the media, you don't have the PR and the marketing, whatever it is. And you've got to start fleshing this out. Don't worry about starting where you're at. Start where you're at and move forward because as you start thinking this way, everything will start to change and the picture will become much clearer. Yeah, so it starts with content. I think a great place to start with content is what you just said is answering people's questions. What it really comes down to is solving people's problems because that's really what every business does. If you're hungry, McDonald's solves that problem. They might not be Morton's, but they solve the hunger problem. Well, what do you solve? Do they want better tomatoes to eat? Well, and you help them grow a better tomato garden. You solve that problem. So just start answering those questions and that becomes your blogs and your videos and all that stuff. But what you now need to do is you need to advertise to get people to that content, which I think is really important in the rollout because advertising is scalable, right? Which, you know, writing blog posts, if I'm the one writing the blog posts, I can only write so many. I can only write, personally, write so many books, but I can buy a lot of ads to send people to that one book, right? Let's talk about scalability for a second because that's one of the biggest mistakes people make. So scalability is the ability to tie things, not just hours for dollars. Even if you're getting paid a lot of money per hour, think about this, at some point, you're the biggest example, the easiest example that I've seen as lawyers, the higher you get paid per hour, the harder it is to take hours off. And you do not ever want to be in that position. I mean, at some point it becomes unpractical to sleep because I'm losing too much money, right? That's an overgeneralization, way out of character, or way out of proportion, but you get the point. So you got to stop tying things that way. So for instance, the way we do, like, okay, so I can publish, when I first started out, I could work with someone to help them get a book written, I could publish the book, I could, and that was one level. Then I started finding other writers who could help them, so I didn't have to do it, started finding designers. And it's really interesting, it's really easy to become scalable in things you're not good at, but when a lot of us are good at a lot of different things, you've got to figure out what can I step back from, and you have to step back from them, or else you're never going to get there, right? So at some point you've got to figure that out, but then you've got to start thinking, okay, like we did, okay, how can we be more scalable? We can put multiple people in one book, okay? Or we can bring in 20 people for one TV shoot. So like my documentaries are one of the least scalable things because a lot of times I have to go out. Well now I'm training other people, it's still directed by me, but I don't have to go out. Okay, and now we're doing group documentaries. And so how do you scale? Scalability is the idea of can I handle one, can I handle a thousand, can I handle 10,000 or a million just by adding more to the front end, to the mark in the funnel? Because what you don't want to be stuck doing is you don't want to be the guy who, there's more business than you can handle, and you never want it to be where, I was talking to some of the, it is a real estate agent, he said, Nick, I would love to grow my business, but right now all that looks like is more work, and I don't want to work anymore, I'm working already too much, and that's not scalable. No, it's not. You've got to find a way to scale your business, and what you do, it becomes fun and freeing because you're not, again, if I talk to you about growing your business 10 times, most people think about doing 10 times the work. And that is not the case. That's not what you want to do, you can't do that. The most interesting question to ask yourself is how do I grow 100 times or 1,000 times, because your human brain instantly goes, well I can't just do 1,000 times more than what I'm doing now. But 10 times more you might try, two times more you're definitely going to try, right? So it allows you to start looking at things differently, but you've got to get out there, and you've got to create a scalable model where you can bring in other people, other resources to do it for you, or again, you're just going to be, it's going to seem like creating more work for yourself, and that's not what you want to do. Now, don't get me wrong, just start this business, you and I, we've all worked our nights, our weekends, we've all done it all, but you've got to start positioning to where you don't have to do it all. Right, and I think it starts with that mindset that we talked about earlier, that you're a marketer first of those services. Once you think, if you think of yourself as a technician, you get bottled down, and well, if I did 10 times more business, I got to do 10 times more work. If you think about, from the marketing standpoint, I can buy 10 times more ads, hire a few more staff members to handle the work. So a dentist, if I'm a marketer of dental services, I can hire more dental assistants and hygienists and people to come in and do it. If I'm a real estate agent, I can become a broker, and I can hire agents under me, and if I know the marketing side, if I understand how to position myself as a celebrity expert, I can bring in a ton of business and have other people execute on the work, and I think that that's probably the most important concept and grasp in the rollout is the marketer first, I drive the business through my personality, and then I allow others to deliver, right? Let's talk in a second about a couple different ways you can scale, but for instance, the documentary example for me is a great one because most people would say, well, if you got to film something, you got to be there, well, no, I get camera guys, I get producers, and now it costs more money if I need to go because I can't do something else, I can't be working on my business, can't be somewhere else speaking, and I still am the director, so I've created a system where I do all the pre-production, I get them comfortable with the story, I handle the post-production with the editing, with the editor, making sure it's still my production, I'm still the director, but if you want me to be the editor, or sorry, I don't do the editing, if you want me to be the director and you want me on site, well, there's an additional fee for that because I can't scale that, but most people don't want to pay the extra fee, and now it all works the way it did before, but I just don't have to go, and I love going, I love hanging out with great people in cool places, and I've got three coming up so that people did pay the extra money for, okay, well, I had to figure that out, what's worth it, how do you do it? But you can scale almost everything, and if what you are doing you cannot scale, you need to quickly figure out what you can scale. So let's talk about growing the business too, so for instance, what I understood, when I was first working with bands, a lot of this goes back to the stuff, because they had the same problem. It's a universal application, yeah. Absolutely, so at the time, websites were really expensive, and websites were really static, but they would have new albums, they would have new concert dates, they'd have all this stuff, and you have to find someone to try to change the pages, and it was cumbersome, right? It still feels that way to many of you out there today, well, you're on the wrong system, I can tell you that. But what I did, I paid a guy, when I was in law school, actually, I instant messaged with a guy in India, a guy in Virginia, a guy in England, and I had them create this platform for me that we could basically pay them some nominal fee to create a duplicate of it, and skin it for the band, essentially, and then we could go in and make the changes, we could add CDs, we could delete CDs, we could, whatever we wanted, we could change it. And that was one of the first things I started learning about a content management system, I didn't just call that, it was a way to manage content. Well, now, with the web, so when we started this business, I had this cool website built that did all this cool stuff, and people kept saying, man, do you do websites? And I was like, no, we don't do that. Well, it didn't take me that long, but too long to figure out that every time you say no, and someone's asking you for something, particularly the same thing, you could be growing your business by offering that service. So we brought in Lindsay, I brought in Lindsay and it said, hey, look, I mean, it was easier than normal because she is Jack's daughter. So we brought her in, but I taught her everything I knew, and we trusted her because she was family. And so I taught her everything I knew. And then she, now, I had a great body of knowledge in web. I now, I mean, I can, I understand the context. You can play in the game. I can play in the game, and I can have an intelligent conversation. When it comes down to brass tacks about actual pieces, I'm out. But the beauty of it, I was able to scale by putting her in, and we created a scalable system there where we can create thousands of websites for clients, and we can run their sites through a content management system just by hiring more employees. You don't have to know coding, you don't have to know design. We need some designers, need some coders, but that's like a, it's a scalable system. So now we got into online marketing, and I don't have to do it. Offline marketing is the same thing. You run the offline marketing business, you're on part of that, and you're great at that. It's a skill set that I could learn, maybe, but you have a gift for it. And so we're growing the business through different verticals, through different areas that we can help people who come in the front door again, maximizing the value of a client over the customer life cycle. Now you've been in a Best Signed book with us, been on TV with us, been in our big print program. Of course, as you build more runs on the ladder, it does make sense to have two or three or 40 Best Selling books. But as a practical matter, people often will say, well, I'll do one of those books every now and then, but what else can I do that's gonna help me grow my business? It's a little bit different, because having five books is not much different than having four or whatever it is, right? And so now, like, well, could you help me implement some of the stuff that grew up in my business? Because again, they knew they trusted us, they would already built that trust. We already spent money to get that customer through marketing and advertising in some way, shape, or form. And now they work with us on different, different facets of their business. And obviously, we are constantly looking for other things we can add to our business that would serve our client better. Now, had we started all those things at once, we probably would have failed. We started the one and we got really to the core. And then as it came out, we got the demand. We didn't just say, hey, you need this now. So you're getting a lot of questions for the things right now. We're getting a lot of questions for figuring out how do I service this, right? So the fact of the matter is that there are lots of ways to expand your business, but you've gotta roll it out. You've gotta get off the couch. You've gotta do something about it. You've gotta build your celebrity brand. And the fastest way to roll out your business and to build your brand is through content and more specifically content with a call to action. What's a call to action? It's something that says, hey, do this now. So sign on or sign up or give me your email address or- Give me money. Give me money, whatever it is. You gotta ask for the sale. If you don't ask for the sale, you're not gonna get it. But you gotta build trust first. You don't wanna be the cheap salesman who asks for the sale right up front. You wanna be the person who builds trust but always giving along the way, giving people the opportunity to take advantage when they're ready to take advantage. Yeah, I love that. And obviously, hopefully you've heard the analogy like going on a date, right? You don't ask for the home run on the first date, right? And Gary Vaynerchuk, new book, Jab, Jab, Jab, Right Hook, kind of same thing. You don't go for the right hook, the first punch in the fight, right? You give them the jab and the jab could be blog post, blog post, blog post, article, sale, you know? But you really do have to methodically work it because it is a give and take and that's really, really important when developing your content. So let's talk about some practical ways that people can find these scalable next steps in their business and really roll it out because people looking at our business are like, well, obviously logical progression was from TV shows to a movie studio or you're a marketing agency so obviously you can do online. But they're like, I'm a dentist. I don't have a secondary service and that might have been insurance like we talked about earlier in the financial world. Maybe it's developing a CPA practice because if you do someone's taxes, you know, their finances, you can then roll them into retirement plans. So there's always these things that are out there that you can do, but how do we help them to find these areas? The most important thing to do is to see what, well, first of all, from a broad base, you can say, what else is my customer prospect already buying? What are they already buying? Now, they're probably, well, we know they're already buying food. We know they're already buying automobiles and that's a little far-fetched but then starting to area down to what kind of makes sense. It's really brilliant. I have a client who owns some car dealerships and then I didn't realize, but now they own a nationwide insurance agency because when you buy a car, you need insurance. So they may, I don't know the laws and what they're allowed to do, but they may be able to incentivize you to go with their agency if you buy the car from them and the insurance, I don't know, again, whatever's legal, I'm sure they're not going to get illegal, but whatever's legal, you take advantage of it. And I was like, wow, that's really smart. And so, you know, I have another client who's a car dealer on the same topic who offers their own underwritten warranties and other things like that. Now, again, it'll have to be set up correctly, but you can do it. If someone's interested in investing in the markets, well, you may want to have a real estate investment brokerage. You may want to have a business investment brokerage. You may want to start a venture fund. I don't know what it is, but think about what else would people who are coming to you? What else might they like from you? And the fact of the matter is just kind of, one of the best examples of this is being a lawyer. When you're a good lawyer, people don't typically understand you only do one thing. You're like, wow, you really handled my divorce really well. Can you now help me get money from somebody who didn't pay me? Well, I don't do that kind of laws, what a lawyer would typically say, which is fine, but if you say that enough times, bring in an associate who does, because typically, again, once you understand the profile of your client, there's gonna be certain things you know they're gonna need. And look, I mean, Virgin's done it really well. Started a lifestyle process in records and out-ear lines, and you know, Richard Branson happens to think way outside the box, he has big dollars, but the point is, whatever people are buying from you, they would buy more, where you'd offer to them. And most of the time, we are afraid to offer them more. You don't want to be, again, you don't want to look like the cheap salesman with 37 watches in the pocket and everything, but you want to see what's a natural progression. And sometimes it's not even, you don't even have to do it yourself, it's just a strategic partnership. If you know someone else is doing it well, it's kind of an affiliate relationship. Hey, if I introduce you to my clients, you introduced me to yours, we need to trade money or value or services or whatever it is, but there's a ton of ways to continue to grow and expand based on that. Yeah, so I want to kind of turn our attention now to something that, whenever we talk about it, we get a lot of interest, because people really, they're like, Nick, I'm a dentist, I'm doing great, but hey, I want to be the guy talking about dentists speaking all over the world, having these books and products. And it's the whole Dr. Oz thing, or Suzy Orman or Dave Ramsey, whatever it is, they want to be that, right? Because they all want some type of level of stardom, and but they also want to help people too, I think this is really what it boils down to. So let's talk kind of about that transformation from being that person in the job, and even if they're a marketer of that, to turning into, well, now I'm an expert and people look to me for that advice, information, and I get paid for the information versus the doing of the thing. Yeah, so ultimately, once you've figured out any successful system, much like this chiropractor did, that's saleable, that system is saleable. So typically what we will see is clients who figure something out in their own business, then you have to market it, but you can then, a dentist, great example, you can become a coach to other dentists. Once that system is working, you've got other dentists using the system, you've proven it, well, you can offer it just to anyone through products, you can offer it through coaching, you can offer it through masterminds, kind of the latter of ascension stuff, you can have different levels of service, you can decide if you want to offer it regularly or through an area exclusive. So do I only want to let one guy in each marketplace, like the chiropractor did with his ad, only one guy in each marketplace, at that point you're selling something that's inherently limited, turf. There's only so much that you're not going to make anymore until we get to Mars maybe, right? But that's going to be a while. So the fact of that is, and before businesses, they're even longer, right? But the fact of the matter is that then that makes it even more valuable just because you're locking other people out in the marketplace, you can charge more for that. Well, once you've got that figured out, you may have enough systems to create a franchise, right? So you may now want to get everyone together who's been paying money to, hey, let's create a brand out of this, and let's all build something that is great, right? And then once we build that brand, well, what else can we go into? So there's a bunch of different levels of it, but once you figure out something that works, there are ways to sell that. Again, through just as simply as recording a product and transcribing it and sending it out, to DUNFU services or Air Exclusive, to logos we're going to share, all that sort of stuff, but it all starts with creating that one system that works. It starts with what we call proof of concept, making sure that concept works. If that concept works in your business, it can work in a lot of other businesses. Completely agree. And earlier, one of the previous modules I talked about, Brian Tracy, when he first started his business, he was literally knocking door to door, selling soap, but he created a sales process and a system that then he started teaching other people how to sell stuff. It was the same system and process. And again, the rollout that he's done is just, I mean, that is, if you're going to model someone, that is someone to model, modeling Dan Kennedy. He really got known for being in the speaker marketplace, teaching speakers how to develop businesses and different things and then rolled out and expanded. So very important there. And the last point that I want to hit home here in this module goes back to one of our foundational principles is that people buy people, right? And especially when you're trying to become that, you know, celebrity expert, that guru, maven, whatever noun you want to put in there, they're buying you because they have a belief in you that you can transform their life or their business, right? When you're watching Suzy Orman, whether you love her or hate her, people buy into her because they, in some way, shape or form, either want to be her or trust her so much that they believe that she can transform their lives. So let's talk about this celebrity expert being this person that people just, they resonate with and they want a piece of you. And that's really what they're buying is you more so than anything else you do. They absolutely are. And one of the things that we do that we'll include in the action guide is kind of this pyramid and reverse pyramid of how you build this up. And then when you think you might have hit the top level of your business, how you can then have a whole other pyramid to start expanding and growing even more. But it's all based off of the fact that you created an environment, you created a world where people want to live. You know, the more you can have ideals, the more you can have aspirations, the more you can get people that, man, I really think like that person, I really want to spend more time with them, I want to be like them. I mean, at the end of the day, people who buy what you offer, at least a little piece of them wants to be like you, if you're doing things correctly. And then the higher up the ascension ladder they go, the more and more they align with you. And I don't mean be like you in a corny way, but like I really like what these people are doing. I would love to do that. Let me see how I can do that with them. And that's ultimately where the big dollars come in from those sort of engagements. Hey, how can we insert what you do into my business? Or how can I align with your business? Or how can I sell what you have or whatever else? Yeah, I think that's really important. So again, going back to, you know, back in the first stages where you're developing your niche and you're developing your story, people really buy into that. And that's what you're really selling. I mean, at the end of the day, when I'm selling my services, I'm selling the fact that people want to be in some way, shape, or form like me, right? When I'm selling my product pros stuff out there, they're like, man, Greg has this great info business. I want what he has, right? It's kind of like the grass is always greener on the other side, but that's really what people, they want to believe that you're that person, right? It really goes back to the earliest roots. You know, think about religion and Christianity. You know, they want the same values that, you know, that were put forth in the Bible and things like that, you know, but in a some way, shape, or form, you're doing that in your business in the way that you create transformation. So as we kind of wrap up here in this fourth step, the rollout, we've talked about a lot of stuff here, but it really comes down to scaling what we've built, right? So we have this one marketplace. Now can we expand to more marketplaces? We have our one story. Can we develop more stories and go into more advertising verticals? We have one core business. What are some vertical or horizontal businesses that we can now develop into? And then how can we take this to the next level and become that expert, that guru, that maven, that celebrity expert that people turn to for advice, information, and transformation? So, a lot of stuff, man. So go forth, go through your action guide, and join us again in the final installment of the Celebrity Branding Blueprint where we tell you how to create legacy dollars with your celebrity brand. Thanks again, we'll see you in the next one.
